Second, Tariffs are another wild card: the $75 million quarterly headwind is surchargeable, but the lag introduces earnings volatility, and any unexpected increase in raw material prices could erode pricing power.

Third, regulatory and competitive dynamics in the truck industry remain fluid. While EPA’27 pre-buy is expected to lift volumes, any shift in implementation timing or technology costs could alter the demand pattern.

Last but not least, any unanticipated change and modification in broader industrial policy risk: trade barriers, subsidy regimes, or shifts in U.S./EU emissions rules could impact both demand timing and margin realization.

Conclusion

Paccar continues to deliver at a level that few others within the industry can keep up with. Q2 was strong, Parts is reporting record revenue, and cost control remains intact. The reality, however, is that deliveries will decline, tariffs are taking a bite out of costs, and industry builds are being reduced. With the stock already trading premium to history and far ahead of global comparables, valuation is capturing much of that excellence. Until we get firmer guidance on tariffs or the EPA'27 pre-buy, it is hard to make the case for fresh upside. I thus have a Neutral rating on PACR.

data.title: Markets Consolidate After The Big Moves Before The Weekend
data.description:
Overview

The US markets responded dramatically to Federal Reserve Chair Powell's speech at Jackson Hole before the weekend. In today's late August session devoid of much news, the markets are consolidating. The dollar is in narrow ranges, and there may be some more corrective upticks in North America today. Emerging market currencies are more mixed. The PBOC set the dollar's fix lower by a relatively large amount (~0.23%) to a new low for the year, and the index of Chinese property developers rose as much as 3% earlier today amid expectations of new measures to support the real estate sector.

The Fed funds futures market is discounting now not only the high probability of a Fed cut next month but also another one before the end of the year and at least three cuts next year. Moreover, the early forecasts for the next US jobs report (September 5) project another weak report and another rise in the unemployment rate. With London closed, the cash Treasury market awaits the US open, but the futures market points to slightly firmer yields. Most European benchmark 10-year yields are 2-3 bp higher. All the large equity markets in the Asia Pacific region report, led by 2% gains in China's CSI 300 and Taiwan's Taiex. South Korea's Kospi and Hong Kong's Hang Seng rallied more than 1%. Europe's Stoxx 600 and US index futures are nursing small losses. Gold rallied almost 1% before the weekend, to almost $3379, but is softer near $3367 in Europe. October WTI has crept up to extend its advance for the fourth consecutive session. It is near $64 after putting in a low last week, near $61.50.

USD: The Dollar Index posted a bearish outside down day ahead of the weekend, in reaction to Fed Chair Powell's comments. It made a new low for the month slightly below 97.60 and settled below the up trendline drawn off the July and earlier August lows. It is trading quietly, consolidating in a narrow range (less than 30 ticks below 98.00). The US economic calendar is light this week, and when everything is said and done, the derivatives market is still pricing in a high probability (~85%) of a rate cut next month and has two quarter-point cuts priced in for this year. Rather than the high frequency economic data, the market may be more sensitive to the Fed officials speaking, including Dallas Fed President Logan, who speaks today, and some have suggested she may be under consideration to succeed Powell as chair. NY Fed's Williams also speaks today. His comments often reflect the leadership of the Fed. Governor Waller, who dissented in favor of a cut last month, speaks on Thursday. He is also thought to be under consideration for the chair, but his unambiguous endorsement of the central bank's independence may jeopardize his chances, especially if Governor Cook's seat becomes vacant. The coming weekend is extended due to the Labor Day holiday, and then the August jobs report comes into focus. The early estimates suggest another soft nonfarm payroll rising by about 85k. The unemployment rate is seen rising to 4.3% from 4.2%.

Euro: This week's highlights include M3 and the lending figures, confidence surveys, and the ECB's inflation expectations survey. The euro is not particularly sensitive to these reports. The euro posted large outside up day ahead of the weekend. It made a new high for the month, near $1.1745. The high from late July was near $1.1795, but the multi-year high set on July 1 was closer to $1.1830. Not only did US rates tumble before the weekend, but the US two-year premium over Germany settled below 175 bp, its lowest since early April. The euro is trading in a narrow range in the upper end of last Friday's range (~$1.1690-$1.1735). The lack of follow-through buying could see some more late longs get squeezed out. Support may be in the $1.1660-80 area. Before the weekend, Germany's Q2 GDP was revised down to -0.3% quarter-over-quarter from -0.1%. The August IFO survey showed a new high in expectations component since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, but the current assessment (86.4 vs. 86.5) has been practically flat for the last several months.

CNY: The PBOC is threading the needle. It has steadily lowered the dollar's reference rate, introduced more flexibility into setting of said reference rate, and the yuan remains broadly stable against the greenback. With a couple minor exceptions, the dollar has been in a CNY7.15-CNY7.20 trading range for nearly three months. Today was another exception. The greenback briefly slipped below CNY7.1490, a new low for the year, encouraged by a sharply lower dollar fix. The PBOC set the dollar's fix at CNY7.1161 (CNY7.1321 before the weekend). The setting of the daily reference rate is one of the chief ways that Beijing manages the exchange rate. Yet, the Chinese hawks want to talk about intervention as if all of the state-owned bank activity is "stealth" activity on behalf of the central bank, as if trade flows (estimates suggest a large portion is still conducted in dollars) and capital flows do not require currency conversion. After surging to CNH7.2240 before the poor US employment report on August 1, the greenback settled into a CNH7.1680-CNH7.1980 range in recent weeks. It was frayed before the weekend, as the greenback set a low near CNH7.1665. After today's fix, it fell to nearly CNH7.1490.

JPY: The hawkish anticipation for Powell and the rise in US yields helped lift the dollar to almost JPY148.80, its highest level since August 1, before Powell spoke. The greenback reversed course and fell to a new low for the week, slightly below JPY146.60. It posted its lowest settlement since July 23. Initial support is around JPY146. In today's consolidation, the dollar has been confined to about JPY146.80-JPY147.55. The corrective pressures do not appear exhausted, but the JPY147.70-JPY148 may be sufficient. The highlight this week comes on Friday: August Tokyo CPI (expected to have moderated) and a drop in July industrial production, while Japanese consumers continued to shop, with a smaller gain in retail sales after the 0.9% jump in June.

GBP: Sterling looked weak. It had slipped below $1.34, for the first time in two weeks. Then Powell spoke and sterling rallied to almost $1.3545. It posted its biggest gain in three months. The high from late July and mid-August, around $1.3600. Sterling has not traded above $1.3535 today and may be headed to the $1.3450-60 area as some momentum traders move to the sidelines. It is a quiet week for UK data, mostly housing data, consumer credit and supply.

CAD: The US dollar rose to CAD1.3925, a three-month high before Powell spoke. It was sold to CAD1.3815 before the weekend. It is in almost a 25-tick range above CAD1.3820 so far today. Nearby support is around CAD1.38, where the (61.8%) of the rally from the month's low (Aug 7 ~CAD1.3720) and the 20-day moving average converge. Still, corrective pressures could see a move toward CAD1.3850-60. Canada is among the last of the G10 countries to report Q2 GDP. The first estimate is due at the end of the week. Bloomberg has two surveys. The first (ECFC) shows a median forecast in its survey of a 0.5% contraction. The second (WECO) shows a median forecast of a 0.7% contraction. The monthly GDP contracted in both April and May by 0.1%. According to the median forecast (WECO) the economy may have grown by 0.1% in June.

AUD: The broad setback for the greenback arrested a four-day slide by the Australian dollar ahead of the weekend. The Aussie had fallen to $0.6415, the low for the month. It proceeded to recover and briefly traded slightly above $0.6500, a four-day high, and settled above the 20-day moving average (slightly above $0.6485). It is one of the best performing G10 currencies today and made a marginal new high to about $0.6505, while holding above $0.6470. Initial resistance is seen in the $0.6510-20 area. Tomorrow's minutes from the Reserve Bank of Australia's meeting earlier this month, when it delivered a quarter-point cut will be looked at for clues about the timing of the next move.

MXN: The dollar had been coiling in narrow ranges against the peso most of last week. Then with Powell's comments at Jackson Hole, the dollar broke lower. It fell to a new low for the week near MXN18.57. It held earlier today, and the greenback has approached MXN18.65. It may not have completed the squeeze higher, but we suspect it has come close and anticipate resistance closer to MXN18.70. Ahead of the weekend, Mexico reported lower than expected inflation figures for the first half of August. The headline rate fell (0.02%) for the first time this year, while the year-over-year pace edged up to 3.49% from 3.48%. The core rate rose by 0.0%, a smidgeon less than expected and the year-over-year rate was shaved to 4.21% from 4.22%. Mexico reports the Q2 current account today. Since the pandemic, Mexico runs a trade deficit (though this year it has swung into surplus). Its current account deficit averaged less than 0.3% of GDP in the past two years and is projected to be about that size this year. The average current account deficit in the 2017-2019 period was almost 1.4% of GDP. As a consequence of US immigration policies and environment, worker remittances (average $5.4 bln a month last year) have already begun to trend lower.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TotalEnergies: A Company In Transition Towards A Green Agenda
data.description:
TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) is a French company fully dedicated to the oil and gas industry. Its main business lines include oil exploration and extraction, primarily offshore, liquefied gas, and LNG. This latter segment encompasses the entire processing and value chain of this fuel, which also includes the midstream and upstream segments. Finally, in addition to the general hydrocarbon business, there is a growing segment dedicated to the production of renewable energy. While this report seeks to provide a comprehensive analysis of the company, I want to draw attention to two points that I consider key in a process that seeks to transition to more sustainable energy.

In this regard, the first change I'd like to mention is the company's quest to increase the share of LNG relative to oil extraction. Second, the longer-term transition to energy generation based on a more sustainable matrix and with a much greater share of green energy.

Regarding the transformation the company wants to undertake, I would like to emphasize, from my perspective, that the changes regarding LNG make sense for the company and adapt it to a reality where environmental regulations are becoming increasingly restrictive, particularly in the case of the European Union. However, regarding renewable energy, the situation is much more complex as it entails a radical change in the company's business model. Transitioning from being an oil & gas company to renewable energy requires a different know-how and expertise than Total has amply demonstrated in recent decades in the fossil fuel segment.

Thesis

TotalEnergies is one of the leading European companies in the oil and gas industry. It currently maintains operations around the world, but primarily with a presence in Europe, Africa, and the now largely neglected South and North America. As can be seen below, just over 70% of its revenue comes from Europe.

It's interesting to note that over the last decade, the company has shifted from a strong focus on oil production to a mixed business, with LNG representing 44% of the company's total revenue in 2024 and oil accounting for 43%. The remaining 13% is accounted for by low-carbon energy production.

From my perspective, the company's guidance regarding the increasing relative weight of LNG production to the detriment of oil is extremely relevant. TotalEnergies expects that by 2030, 50% of its total revenue will come from LNG production and 20% from renewable energy. This, in my view, responds to the European Union's initiatives to ensure a major shift in the continent's energy mix in the coming decades. According to the European Green Deal, launched in 2019, the member countries of the European Economic Community committed to achieving a 50% emissions reduction by 2030. This has entailed a series of measures, regulations, and subsidies by member countries to achieve this goal, which, in my view, is somewhat ambitious.

This quest by Total to increase the weight of LNG relative to oil is closely related to the perspective that natural gas can serve as a bridge in the transition toward a complete restructuring of the global energy matrix. In this regard, I would like to emphasize that the vision of the major developed countries for the world to shift to 100% sustainable energy in the short term has proven to be extremely ambitious. In addition, there is a technical and productive factor, where the change implies longer than expected times. In this scenario, natural gas, which generates far fewer emissions than oil, serves as a great bridge to the goal of a world powered entirely by renewable and sustainable energy.

One of the analyses I find extremely important is the relationship between GDP growth over recent years and the increase in electricity demand. Now, it's worth asking how this increase in global electricity demand is being met. As can be seen, LNG growth has been significantly higher than that of oil. While renewable energies represented a greater increase, it's important to note that the initial level value from which each one starts is much lower compared to LNG. If this trend continues, Total's management's vision for the coming decades is interesting.

I now want to turn to the company's most ambitious plan, which involves an almost complete restructuring over the coming decades. Total expects that in the long term, LNG and oil production combined will represent just 27% of revenue. They estimate the remainder will be distributed between Low-Carbon Molecules and electricity generation, which could represent 50%.

Beyond the fact that the plan not only strikes me as ambitious, while radically changing the company's business model from a conventional oil company to a renewable energy generator, what raises red flags for me is the amount of CAPEX the company plans to allocate to this change of direction.

The project involves a gradual modification of the company, the impact on cash flow can already be seen. While it is true that Total estimates its CAPEX to be $17.8 billion this year, with 34% of that amount allocated to the start-up of new oil and LNG projects, there is an amount that cannot be overlooked. From the company's perspective, approximately 27% of this CAPEX will be allocated to low-carbon energy generation. This percentage represents a total of $4.8 billion, a significant amount that is not distributed to shareholders.

Another point I'd like to highlight is that, relative to the industry, Total's margins are generally lower. It's true that compared to other companies in the S&P 500 or the Euro Stocks 600, the company's margins are very good. However, when measured against industry values, as shown in the SeekingAlpha Profitability segment below, the performance is not as spectacular. In fact, I've previously covered smaller companies that have certainly outperformed.

2Q2025 Results

In this segment, I would like to briefly summarize the company's results reported in Q2 2025 and compare them with those seen in prior periods. Before beginning, I would like to point out that the key financial revenue indicators deteriorated quarter-over-quarter and compared to the same period last year, primarily due to the drop in the international price of fossil fuels, primarily oil.

Regarding the company's reported results, revenues in the second quarter of 2025 were $44.7 billion, a 7% drop compared to the $47.9 billion reported in the previous quarter. If we analyze the trend for the first half of 2025 and compare it with the same period in 2024, we see an 8% drop, virtually the same as on a quarter-by-quarter basis.

A similar trend was seen in Total's EBITDA, which reached $9.7 billion. In the first half of the year, the company's EBITDA reached $20.2 billion. This not only represented an 11% drop compared to the same period in 2024, but when compared to the current EV value, it can be calculated that Total's EV/EBITDA ratio stands at 4.38x. Admittedly, this sounds like a low value, but the recent decline in results, coupled with the plan to modify and invest in alternative energy, could raise orange flags.

I want to now describe that cash from operations (CFFO) showed a value of $6.6 billion in 2Q2025. Compared to the first half of the year, the amount was $13.6 billion, which again represents a decrease compared to the same period last year. If we compare this latest annualized value with the company's market cap, we see that the P/CFFO ratio is around 5.06x, which is an attractive value, but there are companies with much more attractive multiples that are undergoing extremely less ambitious change processes.

Below I share the results of the 2Q2025 earnings release published by the company that stood out the most in financial and economic terms.

Valuation

I now want to move on to a valuation using multiples, using the main European companies in the oil & gas sector as my primary comparables. These peers are found in SeekingAlpha's Peers segment, with the exception of Italy's Eni. For these European companies, I decided to maintain the selection made by Petrobras SA, based on their similar market capitalization, geographic diversification, and similar vertical integration. I deliberately excluded the main American companies, as they show a larger size, a different tax structure, and a greater concentration in North American basins.

The ratios shown in the following table were extracted from SeekingAlpha's own Peer segment. For the analysis, I decided to use PE, EV/EBITDA, P/FCF, and P/BV as valuation ratios. For each ratio, I calculated a target value based on a market capitalization-weighted average, and with that target value, I calculated a target price for each ratio.

Once these values ​​were obtained, and based on a weighted average (the weighting factors of which can be seen in the table itself), I arrived at a valuation of the company. I decided to prioritize EV/EBITDA as a relevant multiple over P/BV because in companies of this size and with so many business lines, the book value can be distorted, affecting this result. From the analysis performed, it can be seen that, compared to its main European peers, Total appears to be trading almost at Fair Value. If we add to this the ambitious reform plan, the HOLD rating I gave the company is somewhat justified.

Risks

Finally, I would like to briefly summarize the potential risks the company could face. Undoubtedly, those tied to the current downward trend in oil prices could generate problems in the short and medium term. The possibility that the new US presidential administration will raise import tariffs, especially considering that China is one of the main victims of these measures, could lead not only to a drop in crude oil demand. What would be far worse would be if the conflict escalated into a trade war between the major economic powers, resulting in a drop in GDP. As we saw at the beginning of the report, energy generation and the increase in demand for LNG and green energy are closely linked to global economic growth.

However, I don't want to go into this point in depth, which I've already discussed with other oil and gas industry companies I've covered, such as Permian Resources and APA Resources, and even in my recent report on YPF and Ecopetrol. What I'd like to highlight is a trend that could mark a path Total could take. Recently, EU countries have allocated increasingly large amounts of money to the development of renewable energy and the growth of these industries. These amounts of money have led to the creation of new companies, but have also been partially allocated to many of the continent's oil companies.

This has led to the initiative for a future of sustainable energy generation by companies that have long been explorers and producers of fossil fuels emerging as a search for this money. The fact that European companies in the oil and gas sector tend to become chasers for EU subsidies poses a potential risk, for me. On the one hand, it's true that revenues increase in the short term because investment projects can be carried out at relatively low costs. But on the other, it creates a dependence on a political incentive that raises doubts about profitability once it is no longer in place. The day countries remove their subsidies, the possibility of projects generating sustainable revenues raises a question mark in my mind.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Capital Southwest: Still A Hold, But Monthly Dividend And Improvements Are Attractive For Income Investors
data.description:
Introduction

As an income investor, BDCs play an integral part in my investment strategy. Previously shunned by investors as BDCs are considered riskier investments, the last few years the sector (BIZD) seems to have grown on them.

This is evident by the amount of analysts on Seeking Alpha that now tout them. When I started here in 2023, it seemed like I was the only one that found them to be not just for short-term income, but for long-term as well.

One reason is likely due to higher for longer interest rates, which have resulted in some strong performances amongst standouts in the sector. However, with increased macro uncertainty, I've become quite cautious of BDCs in recent months.

But Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC), despite uncertainty, continues to impress with each quarter. In this article, I discuss CSWC's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why investors looking to own BDCs for income may consider Capital Southwest.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Capital Southwest back in May in an article titled: Latest Quarter Confirms Why This Is A Top Income BDC.

During their Q4 earnings, CSWC saw their bottom line decline quarter-over-quarter and the previous year to $0.56. Partly, this was due to lower overall base rates and a $0.05 related to the exit of their CEO.

Despite this, the BDCs' performance was solid, as they managed to see double-digit growth in their top line from $178.1 million to $204.4 million.

Although the drop in Nll resulted in dividend coverage falling below 100%, I remained confident in the company's ability to sustain their dividend due to their undistributed taxable income.

Since, CSWC has had a good performance relative to the S&P (SP500), who's up over 10% compared to 8.52% for the BDC.

Impressive Q1 Performance

In recent months, I've grown weary of some BDCs' ability to sustain their dividends. And dividend cuts over the past year, projected lower base rates, and recent reports show the potential for further economic weakening.

Despite me considering most BDCs holds due to macro uncertainty and suspected lower base rates, Capital Southwest has continued to impress me amidst uncertainty.

The most recent quarter saw their bottom line climb $0.05 from the previous quarter to $0.61.

Although pre-tax net investment income is down year-over-year on a per share basis from $0.64, in totality this actually increased slightly from $31.1 million to $31.2 million.

While some investors get caught up on the per share basis, I like to see how a BDC's bottom line grew on a total basis, as this tells more of their credit quality.

For comparison, Ares Capital (ARCC) saw their bottom line decline on a per share and total basis. This retracted from $0.62 to $0.49. And in totality, net investment income fell from $386 million to $342 million.

While I view ARCC & CSWC as two of the highest quality BDCs in the sector, CSWC's performance in recent months has moved them in 1st place of my favorite BDCs, long held by Ares Capital.

And this has to do with how management has impressively recognized gains harvested from their equity portfolio, which continues to be a secret weapon.

During Q1, CSWC saw their undistributed taxable income increase from $0.79 to $1 per share due to harvested gains of $27.2 million. Higher interest and dividend income also played a role in their top and bottom line growth.

Over the last two quarters, gains from their equity portfolio have amounted to $41.3 million. This is how the BDC was able to offset lower investments during the quarter, which amounted to $115 million, down from $146.2 million the prior quarter.

The $115 million was invested across 3 new companies and 12 existing ones, bringing their total to 122 companies. Furthermore, leverage, payment-in-kind income, and non-accruals also improved.

Improved Balance Sheet

Capital Southwest already had one of the lowest leverage ratios amongst its peers, well below the peer average of 1.19x. Leverage improved to just 0.82x, down from 0.89x in Q4.

In comparison to some of my favorite BDCs in terms of quality and performance, only Fidus Investment (FDUS) had a lower ratio of 0.80x.

ARCC's stood at 1.02x. Moreover, Capital Southwest has no maturing debt until October of next year, with only $150 million due. And liquidity was plentiful, with $444 million in cash and cash equivalents.

I touched on this in my previous article, but the BDC received final approval for their second SBIC license, which gives them additional access to capital up to $175 million over time.

So, CSWC seems to be in a favorable position to navigate potential headwinds from a weakening economy or to take advantage of growth opportunities that present themselves.

Monthly Dividend Switch

In June, Capital Southwest decided to switch to paying a monthly dividend, making the stock even more attractive for the long term. The company declared a $0.1934 dividend. And with the switch, I wonder if peers will follow in their footsteps.

While I remain cautious on the sector due to economic weakening, CSWC remains in a comfortable position to maintain its dividend as a result of their robust liquidity in the form of spillover.

As previously mentioned, this increased from $0.79 to $1 per share in the latest quarter, giving them some flexibility should the FED lower interest rates in the coming months.

NAV & Non-Accruals

NAV in the quarter saw an $0.11 drop from $16.70 as a result of restricted stock issuance. On an annual basis, the NAV decline was modest, with a $0.01 decline to $16.59.

Another reason CSWC's latest quarter was impressive is the BDC saw sequential improvements in their non-performing loans on a fair value basis.

This is something I had been critical of in previous articles and suggested, with the economy seemingly weakening, this was something investors should keep an eye out for in the coming quarters.

But these improved to 0.8%, down from 1.7% in the previous quarter. In Q2, these were as high as 3.5%. And the decline is a further testament to CSWC's credit quality, something that has remained resilient during the high interest rate environment.

Premium Valuation

As previously mentioned, the potential for additional economic weakening and lower base rates has kept me cautious on many BDCs.

However, with the way CSWC has performed, seeing improvements in their non-accruals, if I had to suggest one stock to stay invested in that I thought could navigate and avoid a potential dividend cut, Capital Southwest would be it.

However, their premium with a P/NAV ratio of 1.36x continues to keep me at a hold. Moreover, if we do see interest rate cuts this year, the sector could get cheaper as portfolio yields decline as a result.

This would likely cause further dividend coverage tightening, which could result in a potential pull back.

Furthermore, CSWC's current P/NAV ratio is currently one of the highest in the sector in comparison to some of the higher-quality peers:

Hercules Capital (HTGC): 1.62x

Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL): 1.10x

Ares Capital: 1.12x

So, although CSWC is attractive from an income standpoint, I suggest investors wait for a potential pullback before adding, preferably around $20 per share.

Risks & Conclusion

Despite their improvements, economic uncertainty remains high. Moreover, it's likely we could see tariffs start to show up in reports in the coming months.

And although this would likely result in the FED holding rates for longer, this could impact borrowers, causing a spike in non-performing loans as a result.

Moreover, this could also cause PIK income to increase as borrowers could see financial distress from higher for longer interest rates.

While I expect Capital Southwest to successfully navigate due to their financial position, this would likely limit upside and cause their share price to decline.

But if you're an investor currently living off income and are adamant on holding or investing in BDCs, Capital Southwest would be my suggestion.

data.title: Red Violet: Undercovered Stock But Potentially Priced
data.description:
Introduction

In my Nutanix article, I mentioned that I look for technology stocks with the following characteristics: recurring revenue, high gross margins, profitability, and expectation beats. Red Violet (NASDAQ:RDVT) is another company I find excellent on all those fundamental measures. But what's even more surprising is that the stock is simply not given much attention by Wall Street. According to FactSet, there was one analyst actively covering the stock in July and only two this month (August). This means potential future growth may be overlooked by the market, making it a great stock in the long term, given its strong fundamentals and growth prospects. As I detail later, near-term developments seem to be priced in.

Business

Industry

Red Violet primarily operates in the data analytics industry with a focus on fraud detection and prevention. Their technology focuses on providing real-time identification of people (as well as businesses and assets), so I would like to narrow my focus on the fraud detection market as the data analytics industry is quite broad.

As we can see from the chart above, the overall industry is yet to reach its exponential growth stage. As digital transactions continue to become more mainstream, costs from surging cybersecurity fraud continue to rise. This, in turn, increases the demand for preventive solutions, with the North American market projected to grow nearly 200% by 2030. Given that nearly all of Red Violet's revenue is generated in the United States, the industry backdrop is supportive of high-growth potential.

Performance

Red Violet's infrastructure, CORE, is a cloud-native AI/ML-driven platform that supports their two primary customer-facing solutions: IDI and FOREWARN. IDI is an analytics provider that helps organizations in various sectors (finance, healthcare, defense, etc.) to make data-driven decisions. The other platform, FOREWARN, which is also the more popular of the two, is an app-based solution for the real estate sector, providing background information on potential customers. As of last quarter, FOREWARN made up 97.3% of total billable customers.

So how have these services performed? Quite well. In the last six quarters, revenue and EBITDA have beaten expectations, driven by customer growth (as seen above). With a 96% revenue retention rate as of last quarter, Red Violet continues to generate recurring revenue from a sticky customer base, as pricing contracts have represented 74–78% of total revenue since Q1 2024. As time goes on, I would like to see revenue retention climb above 100% and pricing contracts represent around 80% of revenue as further proof of a strong business model. But for now, I think the revenue figures are signs of a favorable growth outlook.

Additionally, Red Violet's strong margins are a sign of steady growth. Often, adding new customers drives up costs and keeps margins stagnant, but this has not been the case so far. Gross and EBITDA margins have been improving consistently YoY. As of Q2, Red Violet reported an all-time high of 84% adjusted gross margin. Healthy margins are a sign of sustainable growth, favorable for long-term prospects.

It's also important to note that Red Violet retains a healthy balance sheet, with $38.8 million worth of cash equivalents and only $2.9 million worth of debt.

For the foreseeable future, FOREWARN continues to be the main focus for management. In their recent Q2 earnings call, management noted they wanted to invest heavily in expanding FOREWARN to new industries beginning in 2026, a logical move in my view given the strength of the platform.

Catalysts

Government Expansion

Management wants to expand into emerging markets where Red Violet currently does not have a strong penetration. This primarily includes the government vertical, an untapped source of large non-cyclical revenue. Back in March 2024, Red Violet appointed Jonathan McDonald (previously at TransUnion) to lead the public sector division and execute on this initiative. Government contracts in identity intelligence usually carry longer integration cycles and higher switching costs, enhancing customer stickiness. Management signaled this expansion to be a key driver in 2026, and I expect government contracts to help drive revenue expectation beats in future quarters.

Partnerships

Given that Red Violet is a smaller name in the IT industry, partnerships make it easier to cross-sell products and reach a larger addressable market. Back in May, Red Violet announced an integration of its IDI solution with Ping Identity to deliver no-code identity orchestration for enterprises' digital workflows, making it easier to use Red Violet's platform. Although IDI is the smaller and less popular segment of Red Violet's brand, it still holds product value since it is backed by the CORE platform. A strategic partnership allows for growth without outsized spending on marketing a less popular product, supporting additional top-line revenue growth without compressing margins. As Red Violet continues to expand into verticals and acquire more customers, I would expect to see more partnerships that would lift revenue through cross-selling in future quarters.

Valuation

The street price target, according to FactSet, is currently $56. But from a relative standpoint, Red Violet has considerably higher multiples than its peers.

Discounted Cash Flow Analysis

Given the company's high multiples, I look at the intrinsic valuation for Red Violet through a discounted cash flow analysis.

From the years 2025-2027, in my analysis, I used CapIQ street estimates for revenue growth, margins, CapEx, and D&A. I project changes in NWC as a % of sales. Given there was no data available for 2028-2029, I used my own projections to reflect my views on the company's growth prospects. I assumed a 16% revenue growth rate in 2028, 2029, a bit higher than former years as I anticipate Red Violet growing in size as it captures a larger market share. For EBITDA margin, I assume it stays steady and continues to follow a gradual but increasing trend of better margins. And for EBIT margin, I assume it grows to 17% by the end of 2029 to reflect the benefits of scale from a larger business in the future. Using these assumptions, I get an implied share price of $39.36, not far, but still below where the stock trades at.

It's important to note that technology companies often trade past their fundamental value. But in this case, I see this as a sign that near-term developments are priced in. Most of Red Violet's near-term growth prospects come from the fact that it is simply growing its revenue and nothing overly complicated (no new products, etc.). However, I do see fundamentals accelerating to where the price is justified or the price comes down near the $39 range, which would make it a good buy. However, since it trades at a high premium to peers as well, I see Red Violet as a HOLD for now.

Risks

Data Security

Given that Red Violet's products have access to enterprises' confidential data, any data breach would harm the brand image of Red Violet, making it hard to gain new customers and hurting existing client relationships. Violations of data security may also go against federal regulation, which could lead to heavy fines and impact overall profitability.

Market Concentration

Most of Red Violet's revenue is concentrated in the US. This means the company is exposed to macroeconomic and regulatory conditions that adversely affect the US, causing Red Violet to fall behind competitors who are diversified geographically.

Conclusion

Overall, Red Violet remains a great business. I would like to see the company develop a larger moat by securing more contracts, particularly with the government, in 2026. However, its current price indicates that it is not exactly the right time to buy, but future price drops (assuming sound business quality) or accelerating fundamentals would push my thesis to a buy.
